trans-unit.source: Historical Impact Assessment (HIA) Sudan exercise
trans-unit.target: Historical Impact Assessment (HIA) Sudan exercise

trans-unit.source: __🔙[Back to Homepage](/content/intro.md)__
trans-unit.target: __🔙[Back to Homepage](/content/intro.md)__

trans-unit.source: A HIA has two purposes: First, understanding in detail what kind of problems are caused by a particular hazard, allows people to make informed decisions on the selection of early actions to counter those problems. Secondly, without a good understanding of which magnitude of flood causes significant humanitarian impact, one can not adjust trigger levels accordingly to tackle those significant events. This exercise is based on the article Historical Impact Assessment (HIA) for Flood in Sudan. In this article, there are seven steps listed for the HIA in Sudan. This exercise will start with step 3- Selecting datasets.
trans-unit.target: A HIA has two purposes: First, understanding in detail what kind of problems are caused by a particular hazard, allows people to make informed decisions on the selection of early actions to counter those problems. Secondly, without a good understanding of which magnitude of flood causes significant humanitarian impact, one can not adjust trigger levels accordingly to tackle those significant events. This exercise is based on the article Historical Impact Assessment (HIA) for Flood in Sudan. In this article, there are seven steps listed for the HIA in Sudan. This exercise will start with step 3- Selecting datasets.

trans-unit.source: Aim of the exercise:
trans-unit.target: Aim of the exercise:

trans-unit.source: In this exercise, you will be introduced to the fundamental principles of this quantitative HIA approach. You will build a simplified HIA dataset with real-world data. Furthermore, you will visualize the produced dataset in QGIS. To analyse the data in QGIS is just one thing you can do with a HIA dataset. The wide range of other options like the production of a timeline infographic will not be covered in this exercise.
trans-unit.target: In this exercise, you will be introduced to the fundamental principles of this quantitative HIA approach. You will build a simplified HIA dataset with real-world data. Furthermore, you will visualize the produced dataset in QGIS. To analyse the data in QGIS is just one thing you can do with a HIA dataset. The wide range of other options like the production of a timeline infographic will not be covered in this exercise.

trans-unit.source: Relevant Wiki Articles
trans-unit.target: Relevant Wiki Articles

trans-unit.source: Data sources
trans-unit.target: Data sources

trans-unit.source: Download the training data folder __[here](https://nexus.heigit.org/repository/gis-training-resource-center/GIS_AA/Sudan_HIA_Ex/GIS_AA_HIA_Sudan_ex1.zip)__ and save it on your PC. Unzip the .zip file! The folder is called __GIS_AA_HIA_Sudan_ex1__ and contains the whole [standard folder structure](https://giscience.github.io/gis-training-resource-center/content/Wiki/en_qgis_projects_folder_structure_wiki.html#standard-folder-structure) with all data in the input folder and the additional documentation in the documentation folder.
trans-unit.target: Download the training data folder __[here](https://nexus.heigit.org/repository/gis-training-resource-center/GIS_AA/Sudan_HIA_Ex/GIS_AA_HIA_Sudan_ex1.zip)__ and save it on your PC. Unzip the .zip file! The folder is called __GIS_AA_HIA_Sudan_ex1__ and contains the whole [standard folder structure](https://giscience.github.io/gis-training-resource-center/content/Wiki/en_qgis_projects_folder_structure_wiki.html#standard-folder-structure) with all data in the input folder and the additional documentation in the documentation folder.

trans-unit.source: Tasks
trans-unit.target: Tasks

trans-unit.source: Step 1: Area of interest ✅
trans-unit.target: Step 1: Area of interest ✅

trans-unit.source: This step is outlined in the article Historical Impact Assessment (HIA) for Flood in Sudan will not be the subject of this exercise.
trans-unit.target: This step is outlined in the article Historical Impact Assessment (HIA) for Flood in Sudan will not be the subject of this exercise.

trans-unit.source: Step 2: Finding Flood Disaster Timeframes ✅
trans-unit.target: Step 2: Finding Flood Disaster Timeframes ✅

trans-unit.source: We want to tie single pieces of information, like impacts, to known flood events in Sudan. Thus, we first need a comprehensive list of such events. In the case of Sudan, there are two sources: EM-DAT and ReliefWeb. [EM-DAT](https://www.emdat.be/) is a disaster database that lists events above a certain severity.
trans-unit.target: We want to tie single pieces of information, like impacts, to known flood events in Sudan. Thus, we first need a comprehensive list of such events. In the case of Sudan, there are two sources: EM-DAT and ReliefWeb. [EM-DAT](https://www.emdat.be/) is a disaster database that lists events above a certain severity.

trans-unit.source: [ReliefWeb](https://reliefweb.int/) is actually an information platform for humanitarian response, but it also has a list of active and past disasters.
trans-unit.target: [ReliefWeb](https://reliefweb.int/) is actually an information platform for humanitarian response, but it also has a list of active and past disasters.

trans-unit.source: Both databases list the same events for the most part. By comparing the two datasets and only selecting unique events, we receive a list of all significant flood events in Sudan and the time frames of each event. This can easily be done in Excel
trans-unit.target: Both databases list the same events for the most part. By comparing the two datasets and only selecting unique events, we receive a list of all significant flood events in Sudan and the time frames of each event. This can easily be done in Excel

trans-unit.source: Task 3: Selecting datasets
trans-unit.target: Task 3: Selecting datasets

trans-unit.source: In this step, we decide which datasets we want to include in the HIA. In this context, there are three sources we can turn to: the Sudan Red Crescent Society (SRCS), ReliefWeb, and FloodList. For this exercise, we will limit ourselves to datasets from ReliefWeb and FloodList. In the 'Input' folder of the exercise data, you can find the folder 'reports_articles.' This folder contains 10 datasets.
trans-unit.target: In this step, we decide which datasets we want to include in the HIA. In this context, there are three sources we can turn to: the Sudan Red Crescent Society (SRCS), ReliefWeb, and FloodList. For this exercise, we will limit ourselves to datasets from ReliefWeb and FloodList. In the 'Input' folder of the exercise data, you can find the folder 'reports_articles.' This folder contains 10 datasets.

trans-unit.source: Your task now is to go through the reports and decide which one you will use in the HIA. Place the dataset you want to use in a new folder.
trans-unit.target: Your task now is to go through the reports and decide which one you will use in the HIA. Place the dataset you want to use in a new folder.

trans-unit.source: As a decision support, you can use these three parameters:
trans-unit.target: As a decision support, you can use these three parameters:

trans-unit.source: __Currentness__
trans-unit.target: __Currentness__

trans-unit.source: The assumption is that later after the event, the picture of what happens becomes clearer, thus the data report is more reliable. This means., if you have multiple similar datasets containing similar data, chose the newer one.
trans-unit.target: The assumption is that later after the event, the picture of what happens becomes clearer, thus the data report is more reliable. This means., if you have multiple similar datasets containing similar data, chose the newer one.

trans-unit.source: __Uniqueness__
trans-unit.target: __Uniqueness__

trans-unit.source: It is important to not only use data from one source because of confidence. The assumption is that different organizations have different capacities and work in different areas; thus, they may have better information in some locations or sectors than other organizations.
trans-unit.target: It is important to not only use data from one source because of confidence. The assumption is that different organizations have different capacities and work in different areas; thus, they may have better information in some locations or sectors than other organizations.

trans-unit.source: Give the datasets you selected an ID. This is essential to be able to later pinpoint from which dataset a particular information was pulled. Change the name of the file to the new ID. Furthermore, create a list of the datasets you want to use for the HIA.
trans-unit.target: Give the datasets you selected an ID. This is essential to be able to later pinpoint from which dataset a particular information was pulled. Change the name of the file to the new ID. Furthermore, create a list of the datasets you want to use for the HIA.

trans-unit.source: Use the method below to come up with the ID’s. Expected outcome:
trans-unit.target: Use the method below to come up with the ID’s. Expected outcome:

trans-unit.source: A folder with all datasets to be used in the HIA. The dataset's names should be their ID.
trans-unit.target: A folder with all datasets to be used in the HIA. The dataset's names should be their ID.

trans-unit.source: A list of all datasets to be used, with ID.
trans-unit.target: A list of all datasets to be used, with ID.

trans-unit.source: Task 4: Preparing Excel table Structure ✅
trans-unit.target: Task 4: Preparing Excel table Structure ✅

trans-unit.source: Before we can start compiling data, we need to prepare the Excel table structure we want to use. Depending on the context the table structure can look differently. In the end, you need to be able to collect information on when something happened, where it happened and what happened. Furthermore, you need to be able to identify from which dataset you take every single information. Below you can find short explanations of the different columns of the table structure.
trans-unit.target: Before we can start compiling data, we need to prepare the Excel table structure we want to use. Depending on the context the table structure can look differently. In the end, you need to be able to collect information on when something happened, where it happened and what happened. Furthermore, you need to be able to identify from which dataset you take every single information. Below you can find short explanations of the different columns of the table structure.

trans-unit.source: Task 5: Data compiling
trans-unit.target: Task 5: Data compiling

trans-unit.source: Finally, we can start to compile the data. Remember we are using the __key + value__ concept to create a long table. Every piece of information gets one row!
trans-unit.target: Finally, we can start to compile the data. Remember we are using the __key + value__ concept to create a long table. Every piece of information gets one row!

trans-unit.source: :::{dropdown} Tipps for data compiling Compiling the data in EXCEL is a time-intensive and repeatable task. Here are some tips to speed up the process:
trans-unit.target: :::{dropdown} Tipps for data compiling Compiling the data in EXCEL is a time-intensive and repeatable task. Here are some tips to speed up the process:

trans-unit.source: __Excel copy function:__
trans-unit.target: __Excel copy function:__

trans-unit.source: Try to use the copy function of Excel as much as possible. When taking info from a map, the information in the columns, year, start_date, end_date, date, Source_ID, and admin_level usually stay the same. So you can just copy and paste this information. In most cases, you should only write the name of location, Impact_type, Impact_quality and Impact_quantity.
trans-unit.target: Try to use the copy function of Excel as much as possible. When taking info from a map, the information in the columns, year, start_date, end_date, date, Source_ID, and admin_level usually stay the same. So you can just copy and paste this information. In most cases, you should only write the name of location, Impact_type, Impact_quality and Impact_quantity.

trans-unit.source: __Use ChatGPT:__
trans-unit.target: __Use ChatGPT:__

trans-unit.source: If you encounter tables that do not fit into your table structure use ChatGPT to turn them into a long table format. For example, look at the image in the table below.
trans-unit.target: If you encounter tables that do not fit into your table structure use ChatGPT to turn them into a long table format. For example, look at the image in the table below.

trans-unit.source: You can copy the content which produces this text:
trans-unit.target: You can copy the content which produces this text:

trans-unit.source: Go to ChatGPT and use a prompt like „Can you turn this information taken from a Table into a long table format?“ + the table as text
trans-unit.target: Go to ChatGPT and use a prompt like „Can you turn this information taken from a Table into a long table format?“ + the table as text

trans-unit.source: Probably you will have to ask ChatGPT to give you the whole table. Something like: "Super good! Can you give me a table on the other states as well? "
trans-unit.target: Probably you will have to ask ChatGPT to give you the whole table. Something like: "Super good! Can you give me a table on the other states as well? "

trans-unit.source: __Use Gemini AI:__
trans-unit.target: __Use Gemini AI:__

trans-unit.source: Google Gemini Can also handle images. For example, it can turn this graph into a table which is much easier to handle than copying everything by hand.
trans-unit.target: Google Gemini Can also handle images. For example, it can turn this graph into a table which is much easier to handle than copying everything by hand.

trans-unit.source: The resulting table can easily be adjusted in Excel.
trans-unit.target: The resulting table can easily be adjusted in Excel.

trans-unit.source: State
trans-unit.target: State

trans-unit.source: Affected People
trans-unit.target: Affected People

trans-unit.source: Deaths
trans-unit.target: Deaths

trans-unit.target: SENNAR

trans-unit.target: S. DARFUR

trans-unit.target: S. KORDOFAN

trans-unit.target: W. DARFUR

trans-unit.source: W. KORDOFAN
trans-unit.target: W. KORDOFAN

trans-unit.source: WHITE NILE
trans-unit.target: WHITE NILE

trans-unit.source: Find a relevant information in a dataset
trans-unit.target: Find a relevant information in a dataset

trans-unit.source: Date:
trans-unit.target: Date:

trans-unit.source: `Year`: This information is mandatory! Check to which year the information is revering.
trans-unit.target: `Year`: This information is mandatory! Check to which year the information is revering.

trans-unit.source: `Start_Date` & `End_Date`: If the information on the timeframe of the flood is available, place the start and end date in these columns. This information should be listed in the Flood Events List.
trans-unit.target: `Start_Date` & `End_Date`: If the information on the timeframe of the flood is available, place the start and end date in these columns. This information should be listed in the Flood Events List.

trans-unit.source: `Date`: If exact information of the date of the __impact__ is available, place it here.
trans-unit.target: `Date`: If exact information of the date of the __impact__ is available, place it here.

trans-unit.source: Source_ID: Write the ID in the `Dataset_ID` column.
trans-unit.target: Source_ID: Write the ID in the `Dataset_ID` column.

trans-unit.source: Location:
trans-unit.target: Location:

trans-unit.source: `admin_level`: Check t which admin level the information is revering to e.g. state, locality, town or camp.
trans-unit.target: `admin_level`: Check t which admin level the information is revering to e.g. state, locality, town or camp.

trans-unit.source: `admin_1`: This information is mandatory! To which state is the information revering?
trans-unit.target: `admin_1`: This information is mandatory! To which state is the information revering?

trans-unit.source: `admin_2`: If avilebel. To which locality is the information revering?
trans-unit.target: `admin_2`: If avilebel. To which locality is the information revering?

trans-unit.source: `admin_3`: If avilebel. To which town is the information revering?
trans-unit.target: `admin_3`: If avilebel. To which town is the information revering?

trans-unit.source: `admin_camp`: If avilebel. To which camp is the information revering?
trans-unit.target: `admin_camp`: If avilebel. To which camp is the information revering?

trans-unit.source: `Impact_type`: Select the fitting impact type from the Impact types list or create a new one.
trans-unit.target: `Impact_type`: Select the fitting impact type from the Impact types list or create a new one.

trans-unit.source: `Impact_quantity`: If quantitative information, place the number here.
trans-unit.target: `Impact_quantity`: If quantitative information, place the number here.

trans-unit.source: `Impact quality`: If qualitative information, place „Yes“ or „No“ here.
trans-unit.target: `Impact quality`: If qualitative information, place „Yes“ or „No“ here.

trans-unit.source: Below you can find some examples of pieces of information from different datasets placed in the table structure.
trans-unit.target: Below you can find some examples of pieces of information from different datasets placed in the table structure.

trans-unit.source: :::{dropdown} Emergency Plan of Action (EPoA) Sudan: Floods 2018
trans-unit.target: :::{dropdown} Emergency Plan of Action (EPoA) Sudan: Floods 2018

trans-unit.source: This is a small extract from Emergency Plan of Action (EPoA) Sudan: Floods 2018 page 4.
trans-unit.target: This is a small extract from Emergency Plan of Action (EPoA) Sudan: Floods 2018 page 4.

trans-unit.source: :::{dropdown} SUDAN FLOOD RESPONSE HUMANITARIAN PARTNERS UPDATE BY STATE #3 (as of 19 Oct, 2020)
trans-unit.target: :::{dropdown} SUDAN FLOOD RESPONSE HUMANITARIAN PARTNERS UPDATE BY STATE #3 (as of 19 Oct, 2020)

trans-unit.source: The map on the first page shows affecte population per state. This information can be extracted.
trans-unit.target: The map on the first page shows affecte population per state. This information can be extracted.

trans-unit.source: :::{dropdown} Humanitarian Bulletin Sudan Issue 35 | 22 - 28 August 2016
trans-unit.target: :::{dropdown} Humanitarian Bulletin Sudan Issue 35 | 22 - 28 August 2016

trans-unit.source: :::{dropdown} Sudan Floods Countinue (FloodList)
trans-unit.target: :::{dropdown} Sudan Floods Countinue (FloodList)

trans-unit.source: The quote below is just one bit of relevant information from the flood list article.
trans-unit.target: The quote below is just one bit of relevant information from the flood list article.

trans-unit.source: __Data extracted from dataset__
trans-unit.target: __Data extracted from dataset__

trans-unit.target: houses_damaged_totaly

trans-unit.source: Task 6: HIA Data Cleaning ✅
trans-unit.target: Task 6: HIA Data Cleaning ✅

trans-unit.source: When creating such a dataset there will be errors like misspellings of names or wrong numbers. To not create faulty analysis products, we need to make sure that our data is consistent. Especially location names are vital in this context since we use these columns to join the data later on a geodata set in QGIS. If for example state names are spelled wrong, this data will be lost. To make sure our data is consistent, we need to clean it. Although this can be done using many different programs like Excel, R or Python, we recommend using [OpenRefine](https://openrefine.org/).
trans-unit.target: When creating such a dataset there will be errors like misspellings of names or wrong numbers. To not create faulty analysis products, we need to make sure that our data is consistent. Especially location names are vital in this context since we use these columns to join the data later on a geodata set in QGIS. If for example state names are spelled wrong, this data will be lost. To make sure our data is consistent, we need to clean it. Although this can be done using many different programs like Excel, R or Python, we recommend using [OpenRefine](https://openrefine.org/).

trans-unit.source: You can do this step task by cleaning the data you compiled in the previous steps or the data from the folder task_6. If you want to use the data from the „task_6“ folder, your principal task is to clean the file „Sudan_Impact_uncleaned“. To check your results, you can compare your cleaned data with the file „Imapct_Sudan_cleaned“.
trans-unit.target: You can do this step task by cleaning the data you compiled in the previous steps or the data from the folder task_6. If you want to use the data from the „task_6“ folder, your principal task is to clean the file „Sudan_Impact_uncleaned“. To check your results, you can compare your cleaned data with the file „Imapct_Sudan_cleaned“.

trans-unit.source: **Year and Columns:**
trans-unit.target: **Year and Columns:**

trans-unit.source: Check if all values are within the expected range of years.
trans-unit.target: Check if all values are within the expected range of years.

trans-unit.source: Ensure that there are no missing or invalid year values becaus we need a year information for all data
trans-unit.target: Ensure that there are no missing or invalid year values becaus we need a year information for all data

trans-unit.source: *OpenRefine Step:* Use the "Text facet" or "Numeric facet" to explore the distribution of values in the Year column. Use the "Text filter" or "Numeric filter" to filter out any rows with invalid or missing year values.
trans-unit.target: *OpenRefine Step:* Use the "Text facet" or "Numeric facet" to explore the distribution of values in the Year column. Use the "Text filter" or "Numeric filter" to filter out any rows with invalid or missing year values.

trans-unit.source: **Date Columns (Start_Date, End_Date, Date):**
trans-unit.target: **Date Columns (Start_Date, End_Date, Date):**

trans-unit.source: Check if all dates are in the correct date format.
trans-unit.target: Check if all dates are in the correct date format.

trans-unit.source: Ensure that there are no inconsistent date values.
trans-unit.target: Ensure that there are no inconsistent date values.

trans-unit.source: *OpenRefine Step:* Use the "Edit cells" > "Common transforms" > "To date" option to convert date columns to a standard date format. Use the "Text facet" to identify and correct any inconsistencies in formatting or missing dates.
trans-unit.target: *OpenRefine Step:* Use the "Edit cells" > "Common transforms" > "To date" option to convert date columns to a standard date format. Use the "Text facet" to identify and correct any inconsistencies in formatting or missing dates.

trans-unit.source: **source_ID Column:**
trans-unit.target: **source_ID Column:**

trans-unit.source: Check that there is only one way of spelling for every individual source_ID!
trans-unit.target: Check that there is only one way of spelling for every individual source_ID!

trans-unit.source: Check if all source IDs follow a consistent naming convention.
trans-unit.target: Check if all source IDs follow a consistent naming convention.

trans-unit.source: Ensure that there are no missing source IDs.
trans-unit.target: Ensure that there are no missing source IDs.

trans-unit.source: *OpenRefine Step:* Use the "Text facet" to explore the distribution of values in each admin level column. Click on `Cluster` and set `Method` to `Key collision`or `Nearest neighbor`. Adjust wrong names by checking `Merge` and adjust the `New cell value` and click on `Merge selected & re-cluster`
trans-unit.target: *OpenRefine Step:* Use the "Text facet" to explore the distribution of values in each admin level column. Click on `Cluster` and set `Method` to `Key collision`or `Nearest neighbor`. Adjust wrong names by checking `Merge` and adjust the `New cell value` and click on `Merge selected & re-cluster`

trans-unit.source: **Admin Columns (admin_level, admin_1, admin_2, admin_3, admin_camp):**
trans-unit.target: **Admin Columns (admin_level, admin_1, admin_2, admin_3, admin_camp):**

trans-unit.source: Check if all administrative units are correctly categorized.
trans-unit.target: Check if all administrative units are correctly categorized.

trans-unit.source: Ensure that there are no misspelled or inconsistent administrative unit names.
trans-unit.target: Ensure that there are no misspelled or inconsistent administrative unit names.

trans-unit.source: *OpenRefine Step:* Use the "Text facet" to explore the distribution of values in each admin level column. Click on [`Cluster`](https://openrefine.org/docs/manual/cellediting#cluster-and-edit) and set `Method` to `Key collision` or `Nearest neighbor`. Consolidate the of states and loclities that they are consisten with the list in the [location chapter](https://giscience.github.io/gis-training-resource-center/content/GIS_AA/en_qgis_historical_impact_assessment_sudan.html#location).Adjust wrong names by checking `Merge` and adjust the `New cell value` and click on `Merge selected & re-cluster`
trans-unit.target: *OpenRefine Step:* Use the "Text facet" to explore the distribution of values in each admin level column. Click on [`Cluster`](https://openrefine.org/docs/manual/cellediting#cluster-and-edit) and set `Method` to `Key collision` or `Nearest neighbor`. Consolidate the of states and loclities that they are consisten with the list in the [location chapter](https://giscience.github.io/gis-training-resource-center/content/GIS_AA/en_qgis_historical_impact_assessment_sudan.html#location).Adjust wrong names by checking `Merge` and adjust the `New cell value` and click on `Merge selected & re-cluster`

trans-unit.source: **Impact Type Column:**
trans-unit.target: **Impact Type Column:**

trans-unit.source: Check if all impact types are correctly categorized and named.
trans-unit.target: Check if all impact types are correctly categorized and named.

trans-unit.source: Ensure that there are no duplicate or missing impact types.
trans-unit.target: Ensure that there are no duplicate or missing impact types.

trans-unit.source: *OpenRefine Step:* Use the "Text facet" to explore the distribution of values in each admin level column. Click on [`Cluster`](https://openrefine.org/docs/manual/cellediting#cluster-and-edit) and set `Method` to `Key collision` or `Nearest neighbor`. Adjust wrong names by checking `Merge` and adjust the `New cell value` and click on `Merge selected & re-cluster`
trans-unit.target: *OpenRefine Step:* Use the "Text facet" to explore the distribution of values in each admin level column. Click on [`Cluster`](https://openrefine.org/docs/manual/cellediting#cluster-and-edit) and set `Method` to `Key collision` or `Nearest neighbor`. Adjust wrong names by checking `Merge` and adjust the `New cell value` and click on `Merge selected & re-cluster`

trans-unit.source: **Impact Quantity Columns:**
trans-unit.target: **Impact Quantity Columns:**

trans-unit.source: Check if all numerical values are within expected ranges.
trans-unit.target: Check if all numerical values are within expected ranges.

trans-unit.source: Ensure that there are no missing or invalid numerical values.
trans-unit.target: Ensure that there are no missing or invalid numerical values.

trans-unit.source: *OpenRefine Step:* Use the "Numeric facet" to explore the distribution of values in each numerical column. Use the "Edit cells" or "Transform" option to handle missing or invalid values, and standardize formatting.
trans-unit.target: *OpenRefine Step:* Use the "Numeric facet" to explore the distribution of values in each numerical column. Use the "Edit cells" or "Transform" option to handle missing or invalid values, and standardize formatting.

trans-unit.source: **Impact Quality Columns:**
trans-unit.target: **Impact Quality Columns:**

trans-unit.source: Check that the same impacts are spelt consistently. For example illness names.
trans-unit.target: Check that the same impacts are spelt consistently. For example illness names.

trans-unit.source: *OpenRefine Step:* Use the "Text facet" to explore the distribution of values in each admin level column. Click on [`Cluster`](https://openrefine.org/docs/manual/cellediting#cluster-and-edit) and set `Method` to `Key collision` or `Nearest neighbor`. Adjust wrong names by checking `Merge` and adjust the `New cell value` and click on `Merge selected & re-cluster`
trans-unit.target: *OpenRefine Step:* Use the "Text facet" to explore the distribution of values in each admin level column. Click on [`Cluster`](https://openrefine.org/docs/manual/cellediting#cluster-and-edit) and set `Method` to `Key collision` or `Nearest neighbor`. Adjust wrong names by checking `Merge` and adjust the `New cell value` and click on `Merge selected & re-cluster`

trans-unit.source: **Delete redundant data**
trans-unit.target: **Delete redundant data**

trans-unit.source: It might be the case that you have for one flood event, multiple information on for example affected populations for the same location. This could later be a problem when you want to analyse the data. Probably, the reddened information is from two different sources. So you can delete the information of the older source and only keep the one from the up-to-date source as possible.
trans-unit.target: It might be the case that you have for one flood event, multiple information on for example affected populations for the same location. This could later be a problem when you want to analyse the data. Probably, the reddened information is from two different sources. So you can delete the information of the older source and only keep the one from the up-to-date source as possible.

trans-unit.source: Task 7: Joining P-Code columns ✅
trans-unit.target: Task 7: Joining P-Code columns ✅

trans-unit.source: Usually, it is easier in QGIS to work with data having P-code columns in contrast to state or district names. There is just less potential for errors. Once you are done with data compiling and cleaning, it is recommended to join p-code columns to your data. Below you can find a method to do that in Excel.
trans-unit.target: Usually, it is easier in QGIS to work with data having P-code columns in contrast to state or district names. There is just less potential for errors. Once you are done with data compiling and cleaning, it is recommended to join p-code columns to your data. Below you can find a method to do that in Excel.

trans-unit.source: Task 8: Data export using Pivot-Table in Excel ✅
trans-unit.target: Task 8: Data export using Pivot-Table in Excel ✅

trans-unit.source: __Impact Quantity for one year on state level:__
trans-unit.target: __Impact Quantity for one year on state level:__

trans-unit.source: Open the Excel dataset.
trans-unit.target: Open the Excel dataset.

trans-unit.source: Turn the data in a table by clicking on `Insert` -> `Table`-> check `My table has headers`
trans-unit.target: Turn the data in a table by clicking on `Insert` -> `Table`-> check `My table has headers`

trans-unit.source: Also under the `Insert`-Tab click on `Pivot Table`. Make sure your table range is correct. Check `New Worksheet`. Click `OK`.
trans-unit.target: Also under the `Insert`-Tab click on `Pivot Table`. Make sure your table range is correct. Check `New Worksheet`. Click `OK`.

trans-unit.source: Setup the pivot table by placing the columns as follows:
trans-unit.target: Setup the pivot table by placing the columns as follows:

trans-unit.source: Filter: Start_year
trans-unit.target: Filter: Start_year

trans-unit.source: Columns: Impact_Type
trans-unit.target: Columns: Impact_Type

trans-unit.source: Rows: admin_1 or admin_1_PCODE (If you want to use this table in QGIS, you should use admin_1_PCODE Instead of admin_1)
trans-unit.target: Rows: admin_1 or admin_1_PCODE (If you want to use this table in QGIS, you should use admin_1_PCODE Instead of admin_1)

trans-unit.source: Values: Impact_quantity
trans-unit.target: Values: Impact_quantity

trans-unit.source: To see the sum of the different impacts click on Impact_quantity under Values -> `Value Field Settings` -> select `Sum`.
trans-unit.target: To see the sum of the different impacts click on Impact_quantity under Values -> `Value Field Settings` -> select `Sum`.

trans-unit.source: Directly above the pivot table, you should see the option to filter by year. Select the year you are interested in. For the following example the year 2020 was used.
trans-unit.target: Directly above the pivot table, you should see the option to filter by year. Select the year you are interested in. For the following example the year 2020 was used.

trans-unit.source: Now you can just copy the whole table, and place it in a new worksheet. Make sure to only paste the values. Save this output as a CSV-file, this will make the import of the subset into QGIS easier. Now we can use this table to join it with an existing geodataset in QGIS.
trans-unit.target: Now you can just copy the whole table, and place it in a new worksheet. Make sure to only paste the values. Save this output as a CSV-file, this will make the import of the subset into QGIS easier. Now we can use this table to join it with an existing geodataset in QGIS.

trans-unit.source: __Flood events in Sudanese states for all the recorded years__
trans-unit.target: __Flood events in Sudanese states for all the recorded years__

trans-unit.source: In this section we want to analyse and visualise all recorded flooding events for all the Sudanese state. With our filter for the Impact-Type we derive information for the years 2003 until 2021.
trans-unit.target: In this section we want to analyse and visualise all recorded flooding events for all the Sudanese state. With our filter for the Impact-Type we derive information for the years 2003 until 2021.

trans-unit.source: In the first step we will create a new `pivot table` and store it in a new sheet. Here we will specify the following:
trans-unit.target: In the first step we will create a new `pivot table` and store it in a new sheet. Here we will specify the following:

trans-unit.source: Columns: Impact_Type
trans-unit.target: Columns: Impact_Type

trans-unit.source: Rows: admin_1_PCODE
trans-unit.target: Rows: admin_1_PCODE

trans-unit.source: Values: Count of impact_quality
trans-unit.target: Values: Count of impact_quality

trans-unit.source: Now we want to filter the columns labels to the following impact types:
trans-unit.target: Now we want to filter the columns labels to the following impact types:

trans-unit.source: Event
trans-unit.target: Event

trans-unit.source: Flash-flood
trans-unit.target: Flash-flood

trans-unit.source: Flood
trans-unit.target: Flood

trans-unit.source: Flooding
trans-unit.target: Flooding

trans-unit.source: We also want to include the Event and Flash-floods as these types can also be associated with flooding events as a result of heavy rainfall, seasonal rainfall, etc. We also want to make sure that we rename the column containing the sum of the flooding events to `Sum flood events`.
trans-unit.target: We also want to include the Event and Flash-floods as these types can also be associated with flooding events as a result of heavy rainfall, seasonal rainfall, etc. We also want to make sure that we rename the column containing the sum of the flooding events to `Sum flood events`.

trans-unit.source: Now we export this excel sheet as a CSV file and import it into QGIS. We will open the `Data Source Manager` and select the `Delimited Text` section. Here we need to do the following specifications ({numref}`HIA_import_csv_floods`).
trans-unit.target: Now we export this excel sheet as a CSV file and import it into QGIS. We will open the `Data Source Manager` and select the `Delimited Text` section. Here we need to do the following specifications ({numref}`HIA_import_csv_floods`).

trans-unit.source: Task 9: Data visualization and analysis in QGIS
trans-unit.target: Task 9: Data visualization and analysis in QGIS

trans-unit.source: Open QGIS and create a new projeckt.
trans-unit.target: Open QGIS and create a new projeckt.

trans-unit.source: Import the file "sdn_adm_cbs_nic_ssa_20200831 — sdn_admbnda_adm1_cbs_nic_ssa_20200831" from the input file in QGIS.
trans-unit.target: Import the file "sdn_adm_cbs_nic_ssa_20200831 — sdn_admbnda_adm1_cbs_nic_ssa_20200831" from the input file in QGIS.

trans-unit.source: __Visualise impact quantity data for one year on state level in QGIS:__
trans-unit.target: __Visualise impact quantity data for one year on state level in QGIS:__

trans-unit.source: Import the csv fille "Sudan_admin1_Flood_Impact_quantity_2020.CSV" from the input folder of task 8 in QGIS by opening the `Data Source Manager` and select the `Delimited Text` section.
trans-unit.target: Import the csv fille "Sudan_admin1_Flood_Impact_quantity_2020.CSV" from the input folder of task 8 in QGIS by opening the `Data Source Manager` and select the `Delimited Text` section.

trans-unit.source: Here you can input your CSV-file and depending on the `File Format` you need to define Costum delimiters or you can just select CSV. In thix case go with semicolon `;`. Always check the Sample Data output at the bottom to see if the import is working as expected.
trans-unit.target: Here you can input your CSV-file and depending on the `File Format` you need to define Costum delimiters or you can just select CSV. In thix case go with semicolon `;`. Always check the Sample Data output at the bottom to see if the import is working as expected.

trans-unit.source: `Record and Fields Options`: Specify that your first record is a header.
trans-unit.target: `Record and Fields Options`: Specify that your first record is a header.

trans-unit.source: `Geometry Definition`: Select `No geometry`.
trans-unit.target: `Geometry Definition`: Select `No geometry`.

trans-unit.source: Click `Add`
trans-unit.target: Click `Add`

trans-unit.source: __Outcome:__ You should now have your "Sudan_admin1_Flood_Impact_quantity_2020" file as table in QGIS.
trans-unit.target: __Outcome:__ You should now have your "Sudan_admin1_Flood_Impact_quantity_2020" file as table in QGIS.

trans-unit.source: To turn the impact data into a geodataset we will join it with the --- layer based on th p-codes. We will use the tool `Join attributes by field value`.
trans-unit.target: To turn the impact data into a geodataset we will join it with the --- layer based on th p-codes. We will use the tool `Join attributes by field value`.

trans-unit.source: `Input Layer`: "sdn_adm_cbs_nic_ssa_20200831 — sdn_admbnda_adm1_cbs_nic_ssa_20200831"
trans-unit.target: `Input Layer`: "sdn_adm_cbs_nic_ssa_20200831 — sdn_admbnda_adm1_cbs_nic_ssa_20200831"

trans-unit.source: `Table field`: "admin1Pcode"
trans-unit.target: `Table field`: "admin1Pcode"

trans-unit.source: `Input Layer 2`: "Sudan_admin1_Flood_Impact_quantity_2020"
trans-unit.target: `Input Layer 2`: "Sudan_admin1_Flood_Impact_quantity_2020"

trans-unit.source: `Table field 2`: "admin1_pcode"
trans-unit.target: `Table field 2`: "admin1_pcode"

trans-unit.source: `Join Type` : "Take attributes of the first matching features only (one-to-one)
trans-unit.target: `Join Type` : "Take attributes of the first matching features only (one-to-one)

trans-unit.source: Name the new file "Sudan_admin1_impact_quantity" and save it in your "result" folder
trans-unit.target: Name the new file "Sudan_admin1_impact_quantity" and save it in your "result" folder

trans-unit.source: Click `Run`
trans-unit.target: Click `Run`

trans-unit.source: __Outcome:__ You should now have the layer „Sudan_admin1_impact_quantity“ in your QGIS. The layer should have all columns from Sudan_admin1_Flood_Impact_quantity_2020 and sdn_adm_cbs_nic_ssa_20200831 — sdn_admbnda_adm1_cbs_nic_ssa_20200831.
trans-unit.target: __Outcome:__ You should now have the layer „Sudan_admin1_impact_quantity“ in your QGIS. The layer should have all columns from Sudan_admin1_Flood_Impact_quantity_2020 and sdn_adm_cbs_nic_ssa_20200831 — sdn_admbnda_adm1_cbs_nic_ssa_20200831.

trans-unit.source: Now we can start creating maps or spatial analysis with our impact data. Let's create a map that shows destroyed houses in 2020. Since absolute destroyed houses are natural numbers, we use the option `Graduated` ([Wiki Video](https://giscience.github.io/gis-training-resource-center/content/Wiki/en_qgis_graduated_wiki.html)).
trans-unit.target: Now we can start creating maps or spatial analysis with our impact data. Let's create a map that shows destroyed houses in 2020. Since absolute destroyed houses are natural numbers, we use the option `Graduated` ([Wiki Video](https://giscience.github.io/gis-training-resource-center/content/Wiki/en_qgis_graduated_wiki.html)).

trans-unit.source: Right-click on the layer “Sudan_admin1_impact_quantity” in the `Layer Panel` -> `Properties`. A new window will open up with a vertical tab section on the left. Navigate to the `Symbology` tab.
trans-unit.target: Right-click on the layer “Sudan_admin1_impact_quantity” in the `Layer Panel` -> `Properties`. A new window will open up with a vertical tab section on the left. Navigate to the `Symbology` tab.

trans-unit.source: On the top you find a dropdown menue. Open it and choose `Graduated`.
trans-unit.target: On the top you find a dropdown menue. Open it and choose `Graduated`.

trans-unit.source: Under `Value` select “Houses_damaged_totally”.
trans-unit.target: Under `Value` select “Houses_damaged_totally”.

trans-unit.source: `Color ramp`: Select a white-to-red color ramp.
trans-unit.target: `Color ramp`: Select a white-to-red color ramp.

trans-unit.source: `Mode`: Equal Count (Quantile)
trans-unit.target: `Mode`: Equal Count (Quantile)

trans-unit.source: `Classes`: 5
trans-unit.target: `Classes`: 5

trans-unit.source: Click `Classify`
trans-unit.target: Click `Classify`

trans-unit.source: Click `Apply`
trans-unit.target: Click `Apply`

trans-unit.source: __Flood events in Sudanese states for all the recorded years__
trans-unit.target: __Flood events in Sudanese states for all the recorded years__

trans-unit.source: In this section we want to analyse and visualise all recorded flooding events for all the Sudanese state. With our filter for the Impact-Type we derive information for the years 2003 until 2021.
trans-unit.target: In this section we want to analyse and visualise all recorded flooding events for all the Sudanese state. With our filter for the Impact-Type we derive information for the years 2003 until 2021.

trans-unit.source: Now we impoer the file "Floods_Sudan_all_years" from the input folder. We will open the `Data Source Manager` and select the `Delimited Text` section. Here we need to do the following specifications ({numref}`HIA_import_csv_floods`).
trans-unit.target: Now we impoer the file "Floods_Sudan_all_years" from the input folder. We will open the `Data Source Manager` and select the `Delimited Text` section. Here we need to do the following specifications ({numref}`HIA_import_csv_floods`).

trans-unit.source: The next step follows the same logic as step 2 in the previous example. We will use geodata that contains information about the states of Sudan and join them with the imported CSV data. We will use the `Join attributes by field value` tool and select the ADM1_PCODE as the table field used for the join. An example is shown in {numref}`HIA_join_floods`.
trans-unit.target: The next step follows the same logic as step 2 in the previous example. We will use geodata that contains information about the states of Sudan and join them with the imported CSV data. We will use the `Join attributes by field value` tool and select the ADM1_PCODE as the table field used for the join. An example is shown in {numref}`HIA_join_floods`.

trans-unit.source: Now we can visualise our results using `Graduated` and selecting the corresponding column of the attribute table `Sum flood events`. Select an appropriate color scheme and start creating your map. Your final product could look like {numref}`HIA_map_floods`.
trans-unit.target: Now we can visualise our results using `Graduated` and selecting the corresponding column of the attribute table `Sum flood events`. Select an appropriate color scheme and start creating your map. Your final product could look like {numref}`HIA_map_floods`.
